critique.summary: The article describes the author's experience taking a pest control technician job to better understand the industry before building vertical SaaS solutions for it. The author highlights challenges in recruitment, licensing, operational inefficiencies, and sales processes within pest control companies, concluding with plans to start a company focused on this niche.
mainPoints: The author took a real pest control technician job to gain firsthand industry experience.
Licensing as a pest control technician is rigorous, typically taking 2-3 months, but the author passed in 13 days using a custom training tool.
Operational challenges included delayed equipment provisioning, cumbersome software systems, and extensive monitoring of technicians.
The author successfully made sales and upsells despite minimal formal sales training and inefficient internal processes.
The pest control industry has a large market size ($30B TAM in the US) but is fragmented and resistant to change.
The author plans to start a company building SaaS tools for the pest control niche after acquiring a local operator.
critique.truthfulness: The article is a personal narrative and reflection on the author's experience working in pest control to inform SaaS development. The claims about licensing requirements, operational challenges, and sales processes align with known industry characteristics such as regulatory oversight and fragmented markets. The $30 billion total addressable market figure for US pest control is consistent with industry reports. However, many details are anecdotal and based on the author's individual experience at one company, so they may not represent the entire industry. The article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, limiting its verification beyond the author's account.
considerations: The article does not provide data or perspectives from other pest control companies or technicians to contextualize the author's experience.
There is no detailed discussion of competitors in the vertical SaaS pest control space or existing software solutions.
Potential regulatory variations by state or region affecting licensing and operations are not addressed.
The financial and operational scale of the company where the author worked is not fully detailed, which may affect generalizability.
The article does not explore customer perspectives or challenges from the pest control clients' side.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the author's decision to work as a pest control technician to gain domain knowledge before building a SaaS product for the industry. Commenters generally appreciate the hands-on approach, discuss the challenges and opportunities of building niche SaaS, and debate the viability and ethics of competing with former employers.

comments.author: btown
comments.text:
> That’s why selling SaaS or AI to this kind of company isn’t for me - I’d rather focus my energy on building a company from my own principles, and hire people who share them from the beginning.

> When I told my manager I was leaving, he said I should start my own company and give him a call when I do. So that's what I'm doing.

I love hearing stories like this, because it shows a way to be a builder without the "venture or nothing" narrative that has pervaded the tech space since the dotcom days.

It is very difficult to make a venture-backed services firm (providing services, not software) that can be immediately profitable, grow sustainably, and outperform competitors with in-house technology that's built for real on-the-ground stakeholders... at a speed that will satisfy venture investors.

But it is more possible than ever ([0]), to do this (in-house tech and all) on a bootstrapped basis - since AI reduces the engineering staff required to build, adapt, and maintain an agile best-in-class solution at single-tenant/single-customer scale. The outcome is at the least a lifestyle business, but with upside that can take the form of anything from franchising to licensing to full-fledged SaaS in the future.

I wish OOP the best of luck, and hope he's found a passion. He could go far with this approach if he ends up following through.

([0] This is not to say there are no barriers to entry. There's privilege in the word "founder," and this is no exception. And the K-shaped economy has left many brilliant would-be founders behind. But at least some barriers are lower than they once were, and that's worth appreciating.)
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced and realistic view of the challenges and opportunities in building niche SaaS businesses, highlighting AI's role in lowering barriers and the importance of sustainable growth.

comments.author: colesantiago
comments.text:
There is definitely money in the pest control SaaS business, mine is running at $2M ARR for a few years now.

There are lots of antiquated operators not having newer technology for pest control, which makes this area lucrative for even $50K MRR.

Go for it!
comments.analysis: This comment offers direct industry experience and validates the market potential for pest control SaaS, lending credibility to the author's approach.

comments.author: pier25
comments.text: Domain knowledge is really the most important in any business. If you're making software for a particular industry you won't get very far without it.
comments.analysis: Emphasizes the critical importance of domain expertise when building industry-specific software, supporting the author's decision to gain hands-on experience.

comments.author: clcaev
comments.text:
I liked that you picked a service that has a relatively low barrier to entry. The real asset are local operators and referrals. Making them more efficient without being controlled by a big company would be a boon for everyone involved.

Consider being a platform coop with regional operators as members. See https://platform.coop/
comments.analysis: Suggests a cooperative platform model that could empower local operators, adding a socially conscious perspective to the discussion.

comments.author: zhainya
comments.text: You took a job as a tech in order to learn about pest control business so you could build a SaaS platform?  Do I understand that correctly?  In the end you decided not to build a SaaS and started your own pest control company?
comments.analysis: Seeks clarification on the author's approach, reflecting a common curiosity about the strategy but does not add new insights.

comments.author: Aeroi
comments.text:
I work as a Boat Captain and I've been building Camera Search for 16 months to provide better tools for tradesmen. It's evolved into a larger platform with multiple clients, but the core use case for me was building a video and photo first agent that is grounded in actual manuals and data and provide better diagnostics, parts, and repair info.

My longterm vision is to be the agent platform for traditional industries, bridging the gap between knowledge work and physical work.
comments.analysis: Shares a parallel experience of building tech tools grounded in physical trades, reinforcing the value of domain immersion.

comments.author: dsalzman
comments.text: Doing something similar. Bought a business in the petroleum equipment service space. Building internal tools for ourselves. Pen and paper still dominates the industry.
comments.analysis: Provides a real-world example of modernizing a traditional service industry with software, supporting the article's premise.

comments.author: system2
comments.text: How long was the employment at the pest company? At any point, did anyone treat you like you were stealing their business? I thought about this approach, but I chickened out many times because of the possible confrontation.
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about interpersonal and ethical challenges when transitioning from employee to competitor.

comments.author: deweywsu
comments.text: This might be a bit of a gold rush of sorts at first, in that the first people to transition from tech to running a small business, whether tech-enabled or not, will find a bigger piece of the pie waiting for their taking.  But as the stream of many others increases over the years, the pie's slices will get smaller as competition for the same market segments increases.  Not trying to paint doom and gloom, just that I'd imagine, as the author says, this kind of white to blue collar shift will accelerate, and as it does, competition will rise, lowering the chance for overall profits.
comments.analysis: Offers a realistic market perspective on the risks of increased competition as more tech workers enter traditional industries.

comments.author: TZubiri
comments.text:
Did I read this correctly? You were on the job for 1 month and you are now starting a competing company?

>when I was leaving my boss told me I should start my own company.

Genuinely or sarcastically?
comments.analysis: Questions the depth of experience gained in a short time and the sincerity of the manager's encouragement, highlighting skepticism.
